author: @kendalcole7440
text: This is a really clever & useful build.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: been looking at getting some kanthal A-1 wire for building an electric kiln for a while now, lol, just another project to add more wire to my invoice ha ha thanks for the quick video on this :)

author: @eeezygoing
text:
Ok guys what's gonna happen with those clip leads at the start of this video lol Looks like possible torture equipment lol Of course that depends on the voltages and currents involved Hahaaha

Anyway, It's great to see you guys having fun.
I'm a great admirer of Robert and his merry men :) Have been following the channel from the beginning. Have learned so much from Roberts videos on chemical reactions and his ability to convey not only science but also philosophy and humanity. It is so very refreshing to have your input to the viewers knowledge base. Have viewed with recent great interest the videos of the success with the super capacitor. You guys deserve all the potential success from your incredible discovery.
If I was living in your country I would be there helping out in an instant. You guys are great!
Edward

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: eeezygoing I'll Ashley reply to his own bit mate - but i just wanted to say you would be welcome - Rob

replies.author: @eeezygoing
replies.text: Thanks Rob.  Very much appreciated.   Edward

author: @deslomeslager
text:
I am using the wires from as broad toaster (which was thrown away as it did no longer work) as a resistance to charge super capacitors and older NiCad batteries. But I imagine I could use those wires as well for cutting foam.
Of course I am only sharing this hoping I am going to give someone else the same idea I had ;-)
I do wonder if it can be used, I measured about 2.4 ohm per 20 cm (8 inch)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: deslomeslager Toaster wire is NiChrome and works fine - we just used this wire as we were sent it as a sample

author: @TheGEODEAHOLIC
text: they have been useing the same principle to cut the cores for fiberglas home built airplanes for decades.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: TheGEODEAHOLIC yes i know - we had one in our metalworking shop at school - this is just how to build one for those interested

replies.author: @ReasonForemost
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith  Thank you very much for the idea, building tutorial and tips.  What are the specs of the plug in  power source you use, AC or DC voltage and amp rating?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: ReasonForemost it's just a 12v wall wart - 1 amp i think - you can adjust length of wire for whatever supply you have

author: @darkobul1
text: Its much more precise if you use templates and just slide with wire around it. Also if wire is not streight you can put one end into drill and hold tight other side with someting and while its twisting it will streighten up all the way. Just dont over do it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Darko Bulatovic two very good tips mate - cheers

replies.author: @darkobul1
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith  Learned from people like you on Youtube. Keep up the good work

author: @darkobul1
text: Funny I have been doing that for days now. Found 4.9 amps to be sweet spot for foam. Making some RC Planes and wings. All thanks to youtube and people who share their knowledge.

author: @niji8310
text: Nice! This reminds me of a post on the fasttech forums last year when a guy took a strand of kanthal wire hooked to his e-cigarette atomizer and used it to cut polypropylene.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Niji lol - a clever fellow lol
